#### **Experiment No**: 9	**Date**:30/04/26

# 

### **Aim :** Implement an algorithm that checks for the existence of an Eulerian circuit that construct every edge exactly once

**Theory:** The present exposition investigates the theoretical foundations of global edge traversals, formally transitioning from the generalized concept of a closed walk to the strict topological constraints of an Euler tour. a trail that systematically traverses every edge of a graph exactly once is defined as an Euler trail. When such a trail is strictly closed—originating and terminating upon the exact same vertex—it constitutes an Euler tour. A graph [![][image1]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=G%20%3D%20\(V%2C%20E\)#2) that structurally permits the existence of an Euler tour is formally classified as an Eulerian graph. The analytical exploration of these structures originated from Leonhard Euler's definitive resolution of the Königsberg bridge problem, inherently establishing the mathematical discipline of network topology.  
The structural existence of an Euler tour within a given graph G is fundamentally deterministic and governed by a rigorously proven topological invariant. The primary theorem states that a connected graph G is Eulerian if and only if every vertex [![][image2]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=v%20%5Cin%20V\(G\)#2) possesses a strictly even degree. The necessity of this even parity constraint is logically derived from the continuous nature of the tour; for any internal vertex v along the traversal, the trail must enter v via one edge and subsequently exit v via a distinct, previously untraversed edge. This continuous motion mathematically mandates that the edges incident to any given vertex are completely exhausted in paired sets, thereby strictly requiring the total degree [![][image3]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=d\(v\)#2) to be an even integer. Consequently, the presence of even a single vertex exhibiting an odd degree fundamentally precludes the formation of an Euler tour, as the traversal will mathematically terminate upon becoming inextricably trapped at the odd-degree node, lacking an available unvisited incident edge for departure.

While verifying the necessary and sufficient conditions for Eulerianity is computationally trivial—requiring merely a global linear evaluation of vertex parity and component connectivity—the deterministic construction of the exact ordered edge sequence necessitates advanced algorithmic methodologies. To solve this traversal problem, theorists frequently rely upon Fleury's algorithm, an elegant constructive procedure designed to systematically trace an Euler tour while strictly avoiding premature termination. The algorithm operates by iteratively generating a valid trail, adhering to the fundamental heuristic that an edge whose removal disconnects the remaining untraversed graph structure must only be traversed if no valid alternative exists. In adherence to the formal procedural sequence delineated in the primary text, Fleury's algorithm is executed through the following proper steps:

Step 1\. Choose an arbitrary vertex [![][image4]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=v_0#2), and set the initial trail as [![][image5]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=W_0%20%3D%20v_0#2).

Step 2\. Suppose that the trail [![][image6]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=W_i%20%3D%20v_0%20e_1%20v_1%20%5Cdots%20e_i%20v_i#2) has been successfully chosen. Then choose an edge [![][image7]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=e_%7Bi%2B1%7D#2) from the set of untraversed edges [![][image8]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=E%20%5Csetminus%20%5C%7Be_1%2C%20%5Cdots%2C%20e_i%5C%7D#2) that is strictly incident with [![][image9]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=v_i#2), in such a way that, unless there is absolutely no alternative, [![][image10]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=e_%7Bi%2B1%7D#2) is not a cut edge of the edge-deleted subgraph [![][image11]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=G_i%20%3D%20G%20-%20%5C%7Be_1%2C%20%5Cdots%2C%20e_i%5C%7D#2).

Step 3\. Stop when step 2 cannot be implemented further.

By strictly delaying the traversal of cut edges within the residual graph [![][image12]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=G_i#2), Fleury's algorithm guarantees that the evolving trail never isolates any untraversed structural components, thereby ensuring the final trail perfectly coalesces into a comprehensive Euler tour.

In the domain of applied computational graph theory, the theoretical axioms defining Eulerian graphs and the constructive logic of algorithmic traversals are heavily utilized to programmatically evaluate complex networks. The structural parity constraint is computationally evaluated by systematically isolating the adjacency list and querying the degree function for all vertices [![][image13]](https://www.codecogs.com/eqnedit.php?latex=v%20%5Cin%20V\(G\)#2); any modulo 2 anomaly instantly resolves the network as non-Eulerian. Concurrently, search heuristics such as breadth-first search are deployed to guarantee that all edges strictly belong to a single connected topological component, fulfilling the spatial prerequisite for an uninterrupted circuit. Should these foundational conditions be completely satisfied, the automated generation of the actual Euler tour inherently relies upon implementing the step-wise logic of traversal algorithms like Fleury's or Hierholzer's, sequentially returning the optimal, exhaustive sequence of structural edges.

### NetworkX functions used

In applied network analysis, libraries like NetworkX abstract these theoretical algorithms into streamlined functions. Based on the logic provided, the following specific tools are utilized:

**nx.is\_eulerian(G)**: A Boolean check that computationally verifies both Euler's conditions. It evaluates nx.is\_connected(G) internally and checks modulo 2 parity for the degree of every node in G.

**nx.eulerian\_circuit(G)**: A generator function that executes Hierholzer's algorithm under the hood. It returns the optimal ordered sequence of edge tuples that satisfy the circuit condition.

**G.degree()**: Returns an iterator over (node, degree) pairs. When is\_eulerian(G) evaluates to false, this property is queried to isolate and diagnose the structural failure (the specific odd-degree nodes).

**nx.is\_connected(G)**: An algorithm (typically utilizing Breadth-First Search) used as a fallback diagnostic tool to ensure the graph isn't fragmented into disjoint subgraphs, which would inherently prevent an uninterrupted circuit.

**Code :**  
\# euler\_in.py (with NetworkX functions)  
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  
import networkx as nx  
def create\_graphs():  
    G1, G2 \= nx.Graph(), nx.Graph()  
    e1 \= \[(i, (i+1)%6) for i in range(6)\] \+ \[(0,2), (0,3), (1,4), (5,2)\]  
    e2 \= \[(i, (i+1)%6) for i in range(5)\] \+ \[(i,5) for i in range(1,4)\] \+ \[(4,1), (4,0), (2,0), (3,0)\]  
    G1.add\_edges\_from(e1); G2.add\_edges\_from(e2)  
    pos1 \= {0: (-1,1), 1: (1,1), 2:(2,0), 3:(1, \-1), 4:(-1, \-1), 5:(-2,0)}  
    pos2 \= {0: (0,2), 1: (1,1), 2:(1,-1), 3:(-1, \-1), 4:(-1, 1), 5:(0,0)}  
    return (G1, pos1, "G1"), (G2, pos2, "G2")  
def process\_euler(G, name):  
    print(f"\\n--- Checking {name} \---")  
    if nx.is\_eulerian(G):  
        print(f"{name} has an Euler Circuit\!")  
        circuit\_edges \= list(nx.eulerian\_circuit(G))  
        circuit\_nodes \= \[u for u, v in circuit\_edges\] \+ \[circuit\_edges\[0\]\[0\]\]         
        for i, (u, v) in enumerate(circuit\_edges):  
            print(f"Step {i+1}: Traversed edge ({u}, {v})")          
        print(f"Final Circuit: {circuit\_nodes}")  
        return circuit\_nodes  
    else:  
        print(f"{name} does not have an Euler Circuit.")  
        odd\_nodes \= \[v for v, d in G.degree() if d % 2 \!= 0\]  
        if odd\_nodes:  
            print(f"Reason: Vertices {odd\_nodes} have odd degrees.")  
        elif not nx.is\_connected(G):  
            print("Reason: The graph is not connected.")  
        return None  
def draw\_result(G, pos, circuit, name, ax):  
    nx.draw(G, pos, ax=ax, node\_color='lightgrey', edge\_color='lightgrey', with\_labels=True)  
    if circuit:  
        edges \= \[(circuit\[i\], circuit\[i+1\]) for i in range(len(circuit)-1)\]  
        nx.draw\_networkx\_nodes(G, pos, nodelist=circuit, ax=ax, node\_color='green', node\_size=500)  
        nx.draw\_networkx\_edges(G, pos, edgelist=edges, ax=ax, edge\_color='green', width=2)  
        ax.set\_title(f"{name} Euler Circuit\\n{circuit}")  
    else:  
        ax.set\_title(f"{name} (No Euler Circuit)")  
def main():  
    graphs \= create\_graphs()  
    fig, axes \= plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))      
    for i, (G, pos, name) in enumerate(graphs):  
        circuit \= process\_euler(G, name)  
        draw\_result(G, pos, circuit, name, axes\[i\])      
    plt.tight\_layout()  
    plt.show()  
if \_\_name\_\_ \== "\_\_main\_\_":  
    main()  
// eluer\_mn.py (without Networkx functions)  
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt   
import networkx as nx   
def create\_graph():  
    G1 \= nx.Graph()  
    G2 \= nx.Graph()  
    G1.add\_nodes\_from(range(0,6))  
    G2.add\_nodes\_from(range(0,6))  
    e1\_edges \= \[ (i, (i+1)%6) for i in range(6)\]  
    e1\_edges.extend(\[(0,2), (0,3), (1,4), (5,2)\])  
    G1.add\_edges\_from(e1\_edges)  
    labels1 \= {edge: f"e{i+1}" for i, edge in enumerate(G1.edges())}  
    e2\_edges \= \[(i, (i+1)%6) for i in range(5)\]  
    e2\_edges.extend(\[(i,5) for i in range(1,4)\])  
    e2\_edges.extend(\[(4,1), (4,0), (2,0), (3,0)\])  
    G2.add\_edges\_from(e2\_edges)  
    labels2 \= {edge: f"e{i+1}" for i, edge in enumerate(G2.edges())}  
    pos1 \= { 0: (-1,1), 1: (1,1), 2:(2,0), 3:(1, \-1), 4:(-1, \-1), 5:(-2,0) }  
    pos2 \= { 0: (0,2), 1: (1,1), 2:(1,-1), 3:(-1, \-1), 4:(-1, 1), 5:(0,0) }  
    return G1, pos1, G2, pos2, labels1, labels2  
def get\_adj(G):  
    nodes \= list(G.nodes())  
    edges \= list(G.edges())  
    adj \= {i: \[\] for i in nodes}  
    for e1,e2 in edges:  
        adj\[e1\].append(e2)  
        adj\[e2\].append(e1)  
    return adj  
def is\_cut(G, e):  
    u, v \= e  
    G\_e \= G.copy()  
    G\_e.remove\_edge(u, v)      
    visited \= set()  
    path\_exist \= False  
    adj \= get\_adj(G\_e)  
    def dfs(root):  
        nonlocal path\_exist  
        if path\_exist:   
            return  
        visited.add(root)  
        if root \== v:  
            path\_exist \= True  
            return  
        for node in adj.get(root, \[\]):  
            if node not in visited:  
                dfs(node)  
    dfs(u)  
    return not path\_exist  
def is\_euler(G):  
    if not nx.is\_connected(G):  
        return False      
    for node in G.nodes():  
        if G.degree(node) % 2 \!= 0:  
            return False  
    return True  
def find\_euler(G, pos=None, name="Graph", edge\_labels=None):  
    if not is\_euler(G):  
        print(f"{name}: Eulerian circuit does not exist")  
        return None  
    curr \= list(G.nodes())\[0\]  
    trail \= \[curr\]  
    traversed\_labels \= \[\]  
    G\_temp \= G.copy()  
    step \= 1  
    while G\_temp.number\_of\_edges() \> 0:  
        edges \= list(G\_temp.edges(curr))  
        if len(edges) \== 1:  
            u, v \= edges\[0\]  
        else:  
            for u, v in edges:  
                if not is\_cut(G\_temp, (u, v)):  
                    break  
            else:  
                u, v \= edges\[0\]          
        e\_label \= ""  
        if edge\_labels:  
            e\_label \= edge\_labels.get((u, v), edge\_labels.get((v, u), ""))          
        traversed\_labels.append(e\_label)  
        display\_items \= \[\]  
        for i in range(len(trail)):  
            display\_items.append(str(trail\[i\]))  
            display\_items.append(traversed\_labels\[i\])  
        display\_items.append(str(v))  
        display\_trail\_str \= ", ".join(display\_items)  
        if pos is not None:  
            plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))  
            nx.draw(G, pos, with\_labels=True, node\_color='lightblue', edge\_color='whitesmoke', style='dashed')              
            if len(trail) \> 1:  
                past\_edges \= \[(trail\[i\], trail\[i+1\]) for i in range(len(trail)-1)\]  
                nx.draw\_networkx\_edges(G, pos, edgelist=past\_edges, edge\_color='blue', width=2, alpha=0.4)  
            nx.draw\_networkx\_edges(G\_temp, pos, edge\_color='black', width=1)  
            nx.draw\_networkx\_edges(G\_temp, pos, edgelist=\[(u, v)\], edge\_color='red', width=4)  
              
            if edge\_labels:  
                nx.draw\_networkx\_edge\_labels(G, pos, edge\_labels=edge\_labels)              
            plt.title(f"Step {step}: Removing edge {e\_label} ({u}, {v})")  
            plt.figtext(0.5, 0.02, f"Trail: {display\_trail\_str}", ha="center", fontsize=9, bbox={"facecolor":"orange", "alpha":0.2, "pad":5})              
            print(f"Step {step}: Removing edge {e\_label} ({u}, {v})")  
            print(f"Trail so far: {display\_trail\_str}")  
            plt.subplots\_adjust(bottom=0.15)  
            plt.show()          
        G\_temp.remove\_edge(u, v)  
        curr \= v  
        trail.append(curr)  
        step \+= 1      
    if pos is not None:  
        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))  
        nx.draw(G, pos, with\_labels=True, node\_color='lightblue', edge\_color='gray')  
        edges\_trail \= \[(trail\[i\], trail\[i+1\]) for i in range(len(trail)-1)\]  
        nx.draw\_networkx\_edges(G, pos, edgelist=edges\_trail, edge\_color='blue', width=2)  
        if edge\_labels:  
            nx.draw\_networkx\_edge\_labels(G, pos, edge\_labels=edge\_labels)          
        final\_items \= \[\]  
        for i in range(len(trail)-1):  
            final\_items.append(str(trail\[i\]))  
            final\_items.append(traversed\_labels\[i\])  
        final\_items.append(str(trail\[-1\]))  
        final\_trail\_str \= ", ".join(final\_items)  
          
        plt.title(f"{name} \- Final Eulerian Circuit")  
        plt.figtext(0.5, 0.02, f"Final Trail: {final\_trail\_str}", ha="center", fontsize=9, bbox={"facecolor":"green", "alpha":0.2, "pad":5})  
        plt.subplots\_adjust(bottom=0.15)  
        plt.show()  
    return trail  
def main():  
    G1, pos1, G2, pos2, labels1, labels2 \= create\_graph()      
    print("Graph 1:")  
    circuit1 \= find\_euler(G1, pos1, "Graph 1", labels1)  
    if circuit1:  
        print("Final Eulerian Circuit (G1):", circuit1)      
    print("\\nGraph 2:")  
    circuit2 \= find\_euler(G2, pos2, "Graph 2", labels2)  
    if circuit2:  
        print("Final Eulerian Circuit (G2):", circuit2)  
if \_\_name\_\_ \== "\_\_main\_\_":  
    main()

**Conclusion:**

### A python program to implement an algorithm that checks for the existence of an Eulerian circuit that construct every edge exactly once was successfully implemented
